concepts.title: Rational Numbers and Their Form
concepts.definition: A rational number is any number that can be written as p/q where p and q are integers and q ≠ 0.
examples: 2/3, −5/7 and 4 = 4/1 are rational numbers.
√2 is not a rational number as it cannot be expressed as p/q with integers.

concepts.title: Number Line Representation
concepts.definition: Rational numbers can be represented on a number line by marking their positions between integers according to their values.
examples: 1/2 lies halfway between 0 and 1 on the number line.
−3/4 lies three‑quarters of a unit to the left of 0.

concepts.title: Equivalent Rational Numbers and Standard Form
concepts.definition: Rational numbers that represent the same point on the number line are equivalent; standard form is when the denominator is positive and numerator and denominator have no common factor other than 1.
examples: 1/2 = 2/4 = −3/−6 are equivalent.
−6/8 in standard form is −3/4.

concepts.title: Operations and Properties of Rational Numbers
concepts.definition: Rational numbers are closed under addition, subtraction, multiplication and division (except by 0) and satisfy commutative, associative and distributive properties.
formulae: a × (b + c) = a × b + a × c

concepts.title: Rational Numbers Between Two Rational Numbers
concepts.definition: There are infinitely many rational numbers between any two distinct rational numbers; they can be found by methods like taking the average.
examples: Between 1/2 and 3/4, (1/2 + 3/4)/2 = 5/8 is one rational number.
1/3, 2/5, 3/7 lie between 0 and 1.

concepts.title: Linear Equation in One Variable
concepts.definition: A linear equation in one variable is an equation of the form ax + b = 0 or ax + b = c, where a, b, c are real numbers and a ≠ 0.
examples: 2x + 3 = 11, 5x − 7 = 0 are linear equations in one variable.
x² + 1 = 0 is not linear, it is quadratic.

concepts.title: Solving Simple Linear Equations
concepts.definition: Solving a linear equation means finding the value of the variable that makes the equation true by performing the same operations on both sides.

concepts.title: Equations with Variables on Both Sides
concepts.definition: When the variable appears on both sides, terms are transposed to one side to combine like terms and isolate the variable.

concepts.title: Reducing Equations to Simpler Form
concepts.definition: Equations involving brackets, fractions or multiple terms can be simplified by expanding, clearing denominators and combining like terms before solving.
examples: x/3 + x/4 = 7 ⇒ multiply by 12 to remove denominators.

concepts.title: Word Problems Based on Linear Equations
concepts.definition: Real‑life situations about ages, money, numbers and measurements can be translated into linear equations and solved.
examples: If twice a number increased by 5 is 17, let number be x: 2x + 5 = 17 ⇒ x = 6.

concepts.title: Polygons and Their Classification
concepts.definition: A polygon is a closed figure with three or more line segments as sides. Polygons are classified by number of sides: triangle (3), quadrilateral (4), pentagon (5), etc.
examples: A four‑sided polygon is a quadrilateral; a six‑sided one is a hexagon.

concepts.title: Sum of Interior and Exterior Angles
concepts.definition: For any n‑sided polygon, the sum of interior angles is (n − 2) × 180°, and the sum of one set of exterior angles (one at each vertex) is 360°.
formulae: Exterior angle sum (one at each vertex) = 360°
examples: A quadrilateral has interior angle sum (4 − 2) × 180° = 360°.
A pentagon has interior angle sum (5 − 2) × 180° = 540°.

concepts.title: Types of Quadrilaterals
concepts.definition: Quadrilaterals include parallelogram, rectangle, square, rhombus, kite and trapezium, each with specific side and angle properties.
examples: In a parallelogram opposite sides are parallel and equal; in a rectangle all angles are 90°; in a rhombus all sides are equal.

concepts.title: Properties of Special Parallelograms
concepts.definition: Special quadrilaterals like rectangles, squares and rhombuses satisfy properties regarding sides, angles and diagonals.
examples: Rectangle: diagonals are equal and bisect each other.
Square: diagonals are equal, bisect each other and are perpendicular.

concepts.title: Constructing Quadrilaterals (SSSDD, SSSAS etc.)
concepts.definition: Quadrilaterals can be constructed when sufficient data like side lengths, diagonals and angles are given, using ruler and compass.
examples: Construct a quadrilateral when two diagonals and three sides are known.
Construct when three sides and two included angles are given.

concepts.title: Steps of Construction
concepts.definition: Constructions follow a systematic procedure: draw base, construct angles or arcs to locate vertices, join points and verify given conditions.
examples: To construct ABCD, first draw AB, then construct angles at A and B and mark points C and D at given distances.

concepts.title: Organising and Grouping Data
concepts.definition: Large data sets are organised in frequency tables and may be grouped into class intervals for easier analysis.
examples: Grouping heights of students into intervals like 130–140, 140–150 cm.

concepts.title: Bar Graphs and Histograms (Intro)
concepts.definition: Bar graphs use bars to show frequencies of distinct categories; histograms represent grouped continuous data with adjacent bars.
examples: Bar graph for favourite subjects; histogram for marks ranges.

concepts.title: Pie Charts (Circle Graphs)
concepts.definition: Pie charts show data as sectors of a circle, where each sector’s angle is proportional to the category’s fraction of the total.
examples: If 25% students like sports, the pie sector is 0.25 × 360° = 90°.

concepts.title: Probability (Basic)
concepts.definition: Probability of an event is the measure of likelihood based on equally likely outcomes, defined as favourable outcomes over total outcomes.
formulae: P(E) = (number of favourable outcomes) / (total number of outcomes)
examples: Probability of getting head in a coin toss is 1/2.
Probability of getting an even number on a die is 3/6 = 1/2.

concepts.title: Square Numbers and Their Properties
concepts.definition: Square numbers are of the form n² and show patterns, such as ending digits and sum of first n odd numbers.
examples: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25 are square numbers.

concepts.title: Finding Squares
concepts.definition: Squares of numbers can be found by direct multiplication, using identities or patterns.
examples: Using (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b² to find (20 + 3)².

concepts.title: Square Roots by Factorisation and Long Division
concepts.definition: Square root of a perfect square can be found by prime factorisation or by long‑division method for large numbers.
examples: Long‑division method for √1936.

concepts.title: Square Roots of Decimals and Estimation
concepts.definition: Square roots of decimal numbers are found by grouping digits in pairs and using long‑division, and approximate roots can be estimated by nearby perfect squares.
examples: √10 lies between 3 and 4 since 3² < 10 < 4².

concepts.title: Cubes and Patterns
concepts.definition: Cube of a number is the result of multiplying the number by itself three times, n³, and cubes show various numerical patterns.

concepts.title: Perfect Cubes and Tests
concepts.definition: A perfect cube is a number that can be written as n³; its prime factorisation contains each prime factor raised to a power multiple of 3.
examples: 216 = 2³ × 3³ is a perfect cube; 108 = 2² × 3³ is not.

concepts.title: Finding Cube Roots
concepts.definition: Cube root of a perfect cube can be found by prime factorisation, grouping identical triples of prime factors.

concepts.title: Ratios and Percentages (Recap)
concepts.definition: Ratios compare two quantities of same kind, while percentage expresses a quantity as a fraction of 100.
examples: The ratio 2 : 3 can be written as 40% and 60% of the whole respectively.

concepts.title: Increase/Decrease Percent and Discount
concepts.definition: Percentage increase/decrease compares change with original value; discount is reduction from marked price expressed as a percentage.
examples: An item raised from ₹500 to ₹550 has increase% = 50/500 × 100 = 10%.
Marked price ₹1000, discount ₹200 ⇒ discount% = 20%.

concepts.title: Profit, Loss and Tax
concepts.definition: Profit and loss compare selling price with cost price; taxes like GST are charged as percentage on the value of goods or services.
examples: CP = ₹800, SP = ₹920 ⇒ profit = ₹120, profit% = 15%.
GST at 18% on ₹1000 is ₹180.

concepts.title: Simple and Compound Interest
concepts.definition: Simple interest is interest on original principal only; compound interest is interest calculated on principal plus accumulated interest.
examples: On ₹1000 at 10% compounded yearly for 2 years, amount = 1000 × 1.1² = ₹1210, CI = ₹210.

concepts.title: Expressions, Terms and Coefficients
concepts.definition: Algebraic expressions are combinations of variables and constants; each distinct part separated by + or − is a term, with a numeric coefficient multiplying the variables.
examples: In 5x²y − 3x + 7, terms are 5x²y, −3x and 7; coefficients are 5, −3 and 7.

concepts.title: Monomials, Binomials and Polynomials
concepts.definition: A monomial has one term, binomial has two terms, trinomial has three, and in general polynomials have one or more terms.
examples: 3x is a monomial; x + 2 is a binomial; x² + 3x + 5 is a trinomial.

concepts.title: Addition and Subtraction of Expressions
concepts.definition: Expressions are added or subtracted by combining like terms with same variable parts.

concepts.title: Multiplication of Expressions
concepts.definition: When multiplying expressions, each term of one expression is multiplied by each term of the other, using distributive law and exponent rules.

concepts.title: Standard Identities
concepts.definition: Certain algebraic identities hold for all real numbers and simplify multiplication and factorisation.

concepts.title: 2D and 3D Views
concepts.definition: 2D shapes have length and breadth; 3D shapes have length, breadth and height. Different views (top, front, side) of a solid may look different.
examples: A cube’s top view is a square; a cylinder’s side view is a rectangle and top view a circle.

concepts.title: Nets of Solids
concepts.definition: A net is a two‑dimensional pattern that can be folded to form a three‑dimensional solid.
examples: A cross‑shaped arrangement of six squares can be folded into a cube.

concepts.title: Faces, Edges and Vertices
concepts.definition: Faces are flat surfaces of a solid, edges are line segments where faces meet, and vertices are points where edges meet.
examples: A cuboid has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices.

concepts.title: Mapping and Isometric Sketches
concepts.definition: Maps represent areas from top view; isometric sketches represent 3D objects on 2D paper with equal scales along three axes.
examples: Isometric dot paper helps draw cuboids proportionally.

concepts.title: Area of Quadrilaterals and Polygons
concepts.definition: Areas of trapeziums, general quadrilaterals and polygons can be found by dividing them into triangles or rectangles.
formulae: Area of trapezium = (1/2) × (sum of parallel sides) × height
examples: A trapezium with parallel sides 6 cm, 10 cm and height 4 cm has area (1/2) × (6 + 10) × 4 = 32 cm².

concepts.title: Surface Area of Cube, Cuboid and Cylinder
concepts.definition: Surface area is the sum of areas of all outer faces of a solid.
formulae: Surface area of cuboid = 2(lb + bh + hl)
examples: A cube of side 3 cm has surface area 6 × 9 = 54 cm².

concepts.title: Volume of Cube, Cuboid and Cylinder
concepts.definition: Volume is the space occupied by a solid.
formulae: Volume of cuboid = l × b × h
examples: A cuboid 5 cm × 3 cm × 2 cm has volume 30 cm³.
A cylinder of radius 2 cm and height 7 cm has volume π × 4 × 7 = 28π cm³.

concepts.title: Volume and Capacity
concepts.definition: Capacity is usually measured in litres and relates to volume; 1 litre equals 1000 cubic centimetres.
examples: A container of volume 2000 cm³ has capacity 2 L.

concepts.title: Powers with Negative Exponents
concepts.definition: Negative exponents indicate reciprocals of the corresponding positive exponent.

concepts.title: Laws of Exponents
concepts.definition: Exponents obey rules for multiplication, division, raising powers and dealing with products and quotients.

concepts.title: Standard Form of Numbers
concepts.definition: Very large or small numbers are written as a × 10^n, where 1 ≤ a < 10 and n is an integer.

concepts.title: Direct Proportion
concepts.definition: Two quantities are in direct proportion if they increase or decrease together so that their ratio remains constant.
examples: Cost of pencils is directly proportional to number of pencils if price per pencil is constant.

concepts.title: Inverse Proportion
concepts.definition: Two quantities are in inverse proportion if one increases while the other decreases so that their product remains constant.
examples: Time taken to complete a job is inversely proportional to number of workers if work per worker is same.

concepts.title: Factorisation by Common Factor
concepts.definition: Factorisation expresses an expression as a product of simpler expressions or factors by taking out common factors.
examples: 6x²y − 9xy² = 3xy(2x − 3y).

concepts.title: Factorisation by Grouping Terms
concepts.definition: Grouping terms in pairs or sets can help factor expressions using common factors in each group.
examples: ax + ay + bx + by = a(x + y) + b(x + y) = (a + b)(x + y).

concepts.title: Using Identities for Factorisation
concepts.definition: Standard identities like a² − b² and (a ± b)² are used in reverse to factor corresponding expressions.

concepts.title: Division of Algebraic Expressions
concepts.definition: An algebraic expression can be divided by a monomial or polynomial using factorisation or long division.
examples: (6x²y − 3xy²) ÷ 3xy = 2x − y.

concepts.title: Cartesian Plane and Coordinates
concepts.definition: The Cartesian plane has a horizontal x‑axis and vertical y‑axis intersecting at origin; points are represented as ordered pairs (x, y).
examples: Point (3, −2) lies 3 units to the right of origin and 2 units below x‑axis.

concepts.title: Plotting Points and Reading Graphs
concepts.definition: Points corresponding to data or solutions of equations are plotted on the plane and information is read from the graph.
examples: The graph of y = 2x is a straight line passing through points (0,0), (1,2), (2,4).

concepts.title: Linear Graphs and Simple Applications
concepts.definition: Graphs of equations of the form ax + by = c are straight lines; they can represent simple proportional relationships and help solve problems.
examples: Distance–time graph with straight line indicates constant speed.

concepts.title: General Form of Numbers
concepts.definition: A number with digits can be expressed using place values as a sum of digits times powers of 10, which helps in exploring divisibility tests and patterns.
examples: For a three‑digit number 100a + 10b + c, where a, b, c are digits.

concepts.title: Tests of Divisibility
concepts.definition: Divisibility rules allow checking if a number is divisible by another without full division, using properties of digits and place values.
examples: A number is divisible by 9 if sum of its digits is divisible by 9.
A number is divisible by 11 if difference between sum of digits in odd and even places is a multiple of 11 or zero.

concepts.title: Number Puzzles and Codes
concepts.definition: Patterns in numbers and algebraic expressions can be used to create puzzles and simple codes.
examples: Guess‑your‑number tricks rely on algebraic expressions simplifying to the original number.
